case_id: Transport, combination truck, long-haul, diesel powered; Transport, train, diesel powered

pi_items.source: Task description field explicitly states process should be named 'Distribution Scenario'
Task description states '1000 ton*km of transport'; USLCI documentation confirms ton*km (tonne-kilometre) is the standard unit for transportation processes
Task description explicitly states 'representing 1000 ton*km of transport'
Task description states 'create a report at ~/LCA_Results/mode_shift_report.txt'; export_result.sh confirms this path

pi_items.verified_value: 80-180 kg CO2e reasonable; US freight trucks emit ~131 g CO2e/ton-km
pi_items.source: US freight transportation data: 0.465 lb GHG/ton-mile = ~131 g CO2e/ton-km. Metadata range of 80-180 kg CO2e per 1000 ton*km encompasses this and allows for variation by truck type and efficiency. European data shows 51-53 g CO2/tkm for modern diesel trucks.

pi_items.verified_value: 80-180 kg CO2e reasonable; range accounts for variation in truck types and efficiencies
pi_items.source: Transportation LCA studies show variation from 51-307 g CO2/t-km depending on truck type (urban delivery vs long-haul). Metadata range allows for this variation.

pi_items.verified_value: 15-50 kg CO2e reasonable; US freight rail emits ~0.048 lb GHG/ton-mile = ~13.5 g CO2e/ton-km
pi_items.source: US Department of Transportation data: freight rail at 0.048 pounds GHG per ton-mile = approximately 13.5 g CO2e per ton-km, or 13.5 kg CO2e per 1000 ton*km. UK data shows 26-27.5 g CO2e per tonne-km. Metadata minimum of 15 kg CO2e per 1000 ton*km is slightly above US average but within range of global rail datasets.

The upper bound of 50 kg CO2e per 1000 ton*km (50 g CO2e/ton-km) is conservative and accounts for less efficient rail systems or inclusion of upstream lifecycle impacts. Consistent with international rail LCA data.

privileged_info_summary: This task uses the USLCI database (U.S. Life Cycle Inventory Database from NREL), a publicly available, peer-reviewed LCA dataset. The datasets referenced—'Transport, combination truck, long-haul, diesel powered' and 'Transport, train, diesel powered'—are genuine USLCI processes. The TRACI 2.1 LCIA methodology is a real EPA-published method for impact assessment. The expected GWP ranges are consistent with real-world transportation emissions: truck 80-180 kg CO2e per 1000 ton*km aligns with US freight truck emissions (~131 g CO2e/ton-km), and train 15-50 kg CO2e per 1000 ton*km aligns with US freight rail emissions (~13.5-27.5 g CO2e/ton-km based on DOT and UK rail data). The task involves creating a process named 'Distribution Scenario' with 1000 ton*km functional unit, comparing modal shifts using real LCA methods and data.
